us-politics.headline: US F-15 Downed Over Iran, One Crew Member Rescued
us-politics.body:
A US F-15E fighter jet was shot down over Iran, with one crew member rescued and a search underway for a second. The incident marks a sharp escalation in US-Iran tensions following American strikes on Iranian targets. Austria has since banned US military planes from its airspace, signaling growing international unease.

International law experts have written to Congress arguing the US strikes may constitute war crimes under international law. Critics from across the political spectrum, including podcasters Joe Rogan and Theo Von, publicly condemned the military action. Supporters of the strikes argue Iran posed a direct threat requiring a forceful response.

A separate opinion piece warns that a more dangerous, militaristic junta is consolidating power from Iran's political wreckage, raising questions about what comes next regardless of how the current conflict resolves.

sources.title: 1 rescued after U.S. fighter jet crash in Iran; search ongoing for crew member
Live updates: US rescues one F-15 crew member after Iran downs jet, searches for second
A more dangerous, repressive junta is arising out of Iran’s ashes
Rogan, Theo Von slam Trump’s move to attack Iran
Austria bans US military planes from its airspace
International law experts: US strikes on Iran may have amounted to war crimes
US pilot rescued from downed F-15E fighter jet in Iran, search for second crew member ongoing

us-politics.headline: Trump's Record Defense Budget Cuts Domestic Programs
us-politics.body:
Trump's 2027 budget request asks for a record $1.5 trillion for the Pentagon, the largest defense spending proposal in US history. The same budget eliminates LIHEAP, the low-income home heating assistance program, and proposes a $10 billion fund for Washington DC construction and beautification projects.

House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries blasted the overall budget direction, saying Trump has 'decimated the bank accounts of the American people.' Republicans have largely backed the defense increases while Democrats focus on the domestic cuts. The budget also comes amid ongoing military leadership turbulence, with Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth firing the Army chief of staff and a new acting chief set to take over.

sources.title: 5 things to know about Trump’s 2027 defense budget proposal
Jeffries: ‘What Donald Trump has done is decimate the bank accounts of the American people’
Who is Christopher LaNeve, set to become acting chief of staff of the US Army
Trump proposes $10B fund for D.C. construction, beautification projects
Trump re-ups push to eliminate low-income heating assistance program LIHEAP
Trump requests record-breaking budget of $1.5 trillion for Pentagon
GOP rep on Hegseth firing Army chief of staff: ‘I will look into it immediately’
Trump seeks $1.5 trillion for defense spending in 2027 budget request

us-politics.headline: Cabinet Shakeup Widens as Bondi Fired, Noem Out
us-politics.body:
The Trump cabinet is in open flux. Attorney General Pam Bondi was fired despite what observers described as consistent loyalty to the Trump agenda, following Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem's earlier departure. Fox News reports pressure is now mounting on other cabinet members, though the White House has not confirmed further changes.

Rep. Eric Swalwell denied that Bondi tipped him off to any sensitive report before her dismissal. A separate bipartisan Senate probe is examining meetings between Kremlin-linked figures and US officials, adding another layer of scrutiny to the administration's foreign policy conduct. The simultaneous pressures of military conflict, cabinet instability, and congressional investigations are testing the administration on multiple fronts at once.

sources.title: Bipartisan senators probe Kremlin-linked delegation's meetings with US officials
Pam Bondi fired despite commitment to Trump agenda: Loyalty not enough!
Trump cabinet shakeup expands after Noem exit, Bondi firing — who’s under pressure next?
Swalwell says Bondi didn’t tip him off on report

us-politics.headline: Alito Hospitalized in March, Supreme Court Confirms
us-politics.body:
The Supreme Court confirmed that Justice Samuel Alito was treated for dehydration at a hospital following a March 20 event. The incident was not disclosed publicly at the time. Alito, 75, is one of the Court's most prominent conservative justices, and any health concerns draw immediate attention given the Court's current ideological balance.

The Court offered no further details about Alito's condition or whether it affects his duties. Separately, a federal judge declined to reconsider a decision blocking grand jury subpoenas targeting Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, closing off one avenue of executive pressure on the Fed.

sources.title: Supreme Court confirms Alito fell ill at March event
Supreme Court Justice Alito treated for dehydration at hospital in previously undisclosed March 20 incident
Judge won’t reconsider decision blocking grand jury subpoenas of Fed, Jerome Powell

au-politics.headline: Iran War Reshapes Australian Politics Ahead of Election
au-politics.body:
The US-Iran conflict has spilled into Australian domestic politics, with Prime Minister Albanese facing pressure to respond to a fuel crisis triggered by fighting near the Strait of Hormuz. Energy Minister Chris Bowen is defending the government's position as petrol supply chains tighten and costs rise for households.

The crisis has also reignited debate about economic reform. Some commentators argue the shock has given Albanese political cover to pursue bolder policy changes, including a potential mining tax. Even Liberal defence spokesman Andrew Hastie has suggested miners could contribute more, signalling rare cross-party openness on the issue.

The Coalition's Angus Taylor faces his own pressure to match voter expectations on cost-of-living relief. Both major parties are scrambling to define their economic credibility before an election that now looks harder to predict.

sources.title: The war is no longer about just the Strait of Hormuz
Taxing times for Albanese and Taylor as parties seek to match voter expectations
Energy minister Chris Bowen on the impact of the fuel crisis - podcast
Trump’s Iran war has woken Albanese up to a new reality. Will it spur him towards ‘ambitious’ reforms?

au-politics.headline: US Fighter Jet Downed as Iran Conflict Widens
au-politics.body:
A US crew member was rescued after a fighter jet was shot down over Iran, marking a sharp escalation in a conflict that has already disrupted global oil shipping. The incident confirms the war has moved well beyond a naval standoff in the Strait of Hormuz.

European leaders including Macron and Germany's Merz are now drawn into the diplomatic fallout, with pressure mounting on Western governments to define their positions. The conflict is reshaping energy markets and security calculations across multiple continents. Australia is not insulated - the fuel crisis is a direct consequence, and the government is under scrutiny over its preparedness.

sources.title: The war is no longer about just the Strait of Hormuz
Live: US crew member rescued after fighter jet downed over Iran
Net Zero nations trapped in mad race for oil

au-politics.headline: Telstra Price Hikes Anger Customers as Costs Bite
au-politics.body:
Telstra has raised prices on key plans, drawing criticism from consumer advocates who describe the increases as unreasonable given the cost-of-living pressures already squeezing households. The telco's starter plan changes are pushing some customers to consider switching providers.

The backlash reflects a broader pattern of frustration with large companies lifting prices during a period of sustained financial stress. Regulators have not yet signalled any intervention. For many customers, the increases arrive alongside rising fuel and energy costs, compressing household budgets further.

sources.title: Wake-up call: how Telstra’s ‘unreasonable’ price rises may cause customers to hang up

au-politics.headline: Queensland Man Jailed for Streaming Child Abuse
au-politics.body:
A Queensland man has been sentenced to 15 years in prison after live-streaming child sexual abuse. The North Lakes man was convicted on multiple offences involving the exploitation of children.

The case highlights the growing use of live-streaming platforms in child abuse crimes and the challenges facing law enforcement in detecting and prosecuting offenders who operate online. Authorities have not disclosed how the offending was detected, but the sentence reflects courts treating such crimes with increasing severity.

sources.title: Queensland man jailed for 15 years after live-streaming child abuse

world.headline: US-Iran War Escalates as Fighter Jet Shot Down
world.body:
Iran shot down a US fighter jet and launched search operations for its crew, releasing footage of American aircraft scouring the area. Iranian missile debris also struck a factory in Israel, widening the conflict's footprint. A Red Crescent warehouse in southwest Iran was attacked amid the fighting.

Trump meanwhile told reporters the US could eventually "take the oil" in Iran, signalling ambitions beyond the current military campaign. Experts disputed the US account of a deadly strike on an Iranian sports hall in Lamerd, raising questions about civilian casualties. Despite the war, US jobs surged unexpectedly in March and unemployment fell, defying fears of economic damage.

sources.title: What we know about the US fighter jet shot down in Iran
Drone footage shows a factory in Israel damaged by Iranian missile debris
Iranian forces launch search for crew of downed US fighter jet
Trump says with more time, US can ‘take the oil’ in Iran
Red Crescent supply warehouse attacked in southwest Iran
Iran release videos of US aircraft searching for pilots shot down in Iran
US jobs surge unexpectedly in March despite Iran war
Experts dispute US account of deadly Iran sports hall strike in Lamerd
US unemployment rate drops despite economic uncertainty and Iran war

world.headline: Trump Seeks $1.5 Trillion Defence Budget, Cuts Domestic Spending
world.body:
The White House submitted a budget request to Congress seeking a historic $1.5 trillion for the military, the largest such ask in US history. The proposal pairs the defence surge with deep cuts to domestic programmes, sharpening a political fight with lawmakers over spending priorities.

Separately, Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth asked the US Army's top general to step down, continuing a pattern of leadership shake-ups at the Pentagon since Trump returned to office.

sources.title: Trump seeks historic $1.5 trillion for military in Congress budget request
Trump seeks $1.5tn for defence alongside domestic spending cuts
Pete Hegseth asks US Army's top general to step down

world.headline: Hormuz Strait Reopens to Shipping After Iran Conflict
world.body:
A French-owned container ship transited the Strait of Hormuz, the first such passage since the US-Iran war began. The crossing signals a tentative reopening of one of the world's most critical shipping lanes, through which roughly a fifth of global oil flows.

Israel separately threatened to destroy additional bridges in Lebanon as tensions on that front continued to mount. Cuba announced it would release more than 2,000 prisoners under mounting US pressure, while Cambodia's parliament passed new legislation targeting cybercrime scam networks operating within its borders.

sources.title: French-owned ship passes through Strait of Hormuz
French-owned container ship transits Hormuz Strait in first since Iran war
Israel threatens to destroy more Lebanon bridges as crisis mounts
Cuba to release more than 2,000 prisoners as US pressure mounts
Cambodia parliament approves law to combat cybercrime scam rings

world.headline: Artemis II Crew Photograph Earth From Deep Space
world.body:
The four-person Artemis II crew captured what NASA described as a spectacular image of Earth as the spacecraft left Earth's orbit, marking a milestone in the first crewed lunar mission in more than 50 years. The mission continues to progress on schedule.

Elsewhere, Italy's Uffizi gallery confirmed a cyber-attack but denied any security breach of its collections data. Greek ministers resigned over an EU farming subsidy scandal. And Italy's football coach Gennaro Gattuso was sacked after the national team failed to qualify for the 2026 World Cup.

sources.title: Artemis II crew take 'spectacular' image of Earth
Watch: Artemis II's journey so far as it leaves Earth's orbit ... in 85 seconds
Italy's famed Uffizi admits cyber-attack but denies security breach
Greek ministers resign over EU farming subsidy scandal
Gattuso out as Italy’s coach after failure to qualify for 2026 World Cup

ai.headline: AI Chatbots Now Prescribing Psychiatric Drugs, Raising Alarms
ai.body:
AI chatbots have crossed into clinical territory, prescribing and refilling psychiatric medications for patients - a development that puts the question of medical liability squarely in front of regulators. The trend reflects how quickly AI is moving from productivity tool to healthcare provider, often faster than oversight can follow.

Meanwhile, OpenAI acquired tech talk show TBPN, signaling a push into media and narrative control beyond its core model business. Google upgraded its Vids platform with Veo and Lyria AI models, adding directable avatars. The week's AI news paints a picture of the technology embedding itself into medicine, media, and creative production simultaneously.

sources.title: Chatbots are now prescribing psychiatric drugs
OpenAI takes on another "side quest," buys tech-focused talk show TBPN
Google Vids gets AI upgrade with Veo and Lyria models, directable AI avatars

ai.headline: Privacy Cracks Widen: Perplexity, Granola, and EU Pressure
ai.body:
A lawsuit accuses Perplexity of running a fake incognito mode that tracks users regardless of their privacy settings - a direct challenge to one of AI search's core selling points. Separately, note-taking app Granola quietly defaults to making notes viewable by anyone with a link, catching users off guard.

At the policy level, critics are warning that the EU is softening its landmark digital regulations under pressure from Washington, potentially surrendering the regulatory leverage it spent years building. Taken together, the pattern is clear: privacy protections, whether technical or legal, are eroding faster than users and governments can respond.

sources.title: Perplexity's "Incognito Mode" is a "sham," lawsuit says
PSA: Anyone with a link can view your Granola notes by default
Critics say EU risks ceding control of its tech laws under U.S. pressure

ai.headline: Crypto Hack and NHS Palantir Revolt Expose Tech Trust Deficit
ai.body:
Solana's Drift Protocol lost $285 million after attackers used a fake token and a governance hijack to drain funds - one of the larger DeFi exploits in recent memory and a reminder that decentralized governance mechanisms remain a serious attack surface.

In the UK, NHS staff are refusing to use the Federated Data Platform over ethical concerns about its Palantir underpinnings, with some workers citing fears about data sovereignty and corporate access to patient records. Both stories point to the same underlying tension: institutions are deploying powerful technology faster than they can build the trust required to sustain it.

sources.title: Solana Drift Protocol drained of $285M via fake token and governance hijack
NHS staff refusing to use FDP over Palantir ethical concerns

ai.headline: Astronauts Head to Moon as Space Race Rivalries Intensify
ai.body:
Four astronauts are en route to the Moon, marking a concrete milestone in NASA's Artemis program after years of delays. The mission puts human lunar exploration back on the immediate agenda rather than the distant horizon.

On the commercial side, SpaceX is pressing the FCC to intervene against Amazon's Kuiper satellite deployments, arguing the altitude choices risk collisions with Starlink. The dispute is less about safety than market position - two well-funded competitors using regulatory bodies as a proxy battleground for low-Earth orbit dominance.

sources.title: Four astronauts are now inexorably bound for the Moon
SpaceX tries to convince FCC that Amazon put satellites into wrong altitude

btc.headline: Bitcoin Slips Below $70K as Corporate Treasuries Diverge
btc.body:
Bitcoin dipped below $70,000 this week, exposing a growing split among corporate holders. Some treasury strategies are holding firm while others are quietly reducing exposure, signaling that the unified institutional front of 2024 is fracturing under price pressure.

Futures markets are tilting bearish and volatility has cooled, with the CoinDesk 20 showing altcoins outperforming a flat BTC. Cathie Wood remains bullish, arguing Bitcoin is done with its historic 85% drawdown cycles and setting a $34,000 floor as a base case. Markets are not convinced. Price prediction models for April 3 show BTC struggling to reclaim momentum across major pairs.

sources.title: Crypto Biz: Bitcoin treasuries break ranks as BTC dips below $70K
Price predictions 4/3: BTC, ETH, BNB, XRP, SOL, DOGE, HYPE, ADA, BCH, LINK
Bitcoin 'done' with 85% crashes, says Cathie Wood amid new $34K target
Crypto consolidates as volatility cools and futures markets tilt bearish

btc.headline: Circle Faces $420M Compliance Accusations as DeFi Hacks Mount
btc.body:
On-chain investigator ZachXBT has accused Circle of $420 million in compliance failures since 2022, alleging the USDC issuer failed to freeze funds linked to illicit activity in a timely manner. The accusations land at a sensitive moment, with Circle pursuing a public listing and positioning itself as a regulated, trustworthy stablecoin operator.

The broader security picture is grim. Crypto hackers stole $169 million across 34 DeFi protocols in Q1 2026, according to DefiLlama. Drift Protocol separately sent an on-chain message to wallets tied to a $280 million exploit, attempting direct contact with the attacker. Analysts argue execution risk has now overtaken custody risk as the primary threat facing DeFi users.

sources.title: ZachXBT accuses Circle of $420M in 'compliance failures' since 2022
Execution risk in crypto is the new custody risk
Drift sends onchain message to wallets tied to $280M exploit
Crypto hackers steal $169M from 34 DeFi protocols in Q1: DefiLlama

btc.headline: Ethereum Foundation Stakes $93M as L2 Scaling Debate Heats Up
btc.body:
The Ethereum Foundation completed a staking milestone, depositing another $93 million in ETH to hit its 70,000 ETH target. The move signals a more active treasury posture from an organization historically cautious about market influence.

Offchain Labs, the team behind Arbitrum, argued this week that Ethereum Layer 2 networks need responsive, dynamic pricing to scale to billions of users. Static fee models create bottlenecks under load. The debate matters as L2s compete aggressively for users and liquidity, with Hyperliquid cracking the top 10 in crypto derivatives volume during Q1, according to CoinGlass data that also showed Binance retaining its dominant position.

sources.title: Binance led Q1 crypto derivatives as Hyperliquid cracked top 10: CoinGlass
Ethereum L2s need responsive pricing to scale, says Offchain Labs
Ethereum Foundation stakes another $93 million ether, reaching its 70,000 ETH target

btc.headline: Quantum-Resistant Chain Launches as Q-Day Fears Grow
btc.body:
Naoris Protocol brought its quantum-resistant blockchain live this week, targeting a vulnerability that most of the industry is still treating as a distant problem. Bitcoin and Ethereum both rely on cryptographic standards that sufficiently powerful quantum computers could break. Naoris is betting that timeline is shorter than consensus assumes.

The launch coincides with rising institutional anxiety around so-called Q-Day scenarios. No credible quantum threat is imminent, but the window for migrating legacy infrastructure is narrowing as hardware advances accelerate. Naoris positions itself as infrastructure for enterprises that cannot afford to wait for Bitcoin or Ethereum to upgrade their own cryptographic foundations.

sources.title: Naoris Protocol's quantum-resistant blockchain goes live as Bitcoin and Ethereum face 'Q-Day' threats

food.name: Miso-Glazed Black Cod
food.description: Silky, caramelised fish fillets marinated in white miso, mirin, and sake then broiled until lacquered and golden
food.tip: Marinate the cod for at least 24 hours — the longer fermentation time transforms the texture and deepens the umami far beyond a quick soak

exercise.name: Farmer's Carry Ladder
exercise.description: Walk a fixed distance carrying heavy weights at your sides, increasing load each round, building grip strength, core stability, and cardiovascular endurance in one brutally simple session

learn.topic: The Ship of Theseus and Personal Identity
learn.description: The ancient paradox asks whether an object that has had all its components replaced remains fundamentally the same object — and when applied to humans, whose cells, beliefs, and memories constantly change, it becomes a genuine puzzle about what 'you' even means across time.
learn.why: On a Friday it's worth sitting with the idea that the person who started this week and the one ending it are subtly different — philosophers from Heraclitus to Parfit have built entire careers on this, and one hour down this rabbit hole will permanently change how you think about continuity, memory, and selfhood
